behaviors.description: A request whose signature does not match its body is rejected with a 401 response; the body is not forwarded.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is handler-to-verifier-to-queue wiring: reject path must both set 401 and prevent enqueue side effect. Unit tests of verifier alone miss handoff bugs. No real OS/network/subprocess is required; no AI/ML dependency.
behaviors.test_description: Invoke the webhook handler through its HTTP layer in-process with an invalid `X-Signature-256` over a known raw body, using a spy/fake queue module. Assert status 401 and assert queue `enqueue` was never called.

behaviors.description: An event is written to the persistent queue before the HTTP 200 acknowledgment is returned to the sender.
behaviors.justification: This is an ordering contract across modules (HTTP handler and queue persistence API). Unit-level logic cannot prove cross-module sequencing. Real network/OS is not necessary if response completion and queue write completion are observable in-process.
behaviors.test_description: Use an in-process server + controllable queue adapter that records when durable write resolves. Send a valid webhook and capture timestamps/order markers for `queue.writeResolved` and `responseSent`; assert `writeResolved` happens first, then HTTP 200.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Rejected System: behavior text requires write-before-ack ordering, not real network conditions.

behaviors.description: The handler returns the HTTP response without waiting for downstream processing of the event to finish.
behaviors.justification: Failure appears at handoff boundary between enqueue/dispatch and downstream worker completion. A unit test of one function won’t catch accidental `await` on downstream processing. No real OS/network needed.
behaviors.test_description: Wire handler with a downstream processor that blocks on a deferred promise. Send a valid event and assert HTTP response is returned promptly while the processor promise is still unresolved; then resolve and verify processing completes later.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Rejected System: real network is not required to detect accidental blocking on downstream promise.

behaviors.description: The response arrives within 3 seconds wall-clock time even when downstream processing of earlier events is slow and concurrent.
behaviors.justification: This is a real-time SLA under concurrency; failures often arise from real HTTP stack, socket backlog, event-loop scheduling, and process-level contention. In-process fakes can hide these latency effects, so mocking can miss real failures.
behaviors.test_description: Run the service as a real process, drive concurrent HTTP requests against it, and keep earlier events' downstream workers intentionally slow. Measure end-to-end wall-clock for new incoming webhook responses; assert each acknowledgment is returned within 3 seconds.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Rejected Integration: behavior explicitly requires wall-clock latency under concurrent load, which is where real runtime/HTTP effects appear.

behaviors.description: An event whose ID was already received is not written to the queue a second time.
behaviors.justification: Dedup correctness is stateful across requests and module boundaries (handler + dedup store/queue write path). Unit-only logic misses persistence/state handoff bugs. No real OS/network/subprocess is required at minimum.
behaviors.test_description: With a real local persistence layer used by the queue module (or equivalent in-process store), submit the same event ID twice through the handler. Assert first request writes once and second request does not create another queue record.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Rejected System: duplicate suppression can be validated with in-process module wiring and local persistence.

behaviors.description: When the queue reports it is full, the handler returns 503 with a Retry-After header.
behaviors.justification: Behavior tests mapping of queue-backpressure signal to HTTP response contract. This is cross-module wiring, not pure unit logic. Real infrastructure is unnecessary because the trigger is explicit queue status reporting.
behaviors.test_description: Configure queue module to return a `full`/capacity error on enqueue. Send a valid webhook through the handler and assert status 503 plus `Retry-After` header presence/value per contract.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Rejected System: behavior is about response translation when queue reports full, not proving real capacity mechanics.

behaviors.description: The persistent queue actually enforces its configured capacity limit and rejects events that would exceed it.
behaviors.justification: This targets queue component + persistence behavior (capacity enforcement), which needs real module wiring and storage semantics, not just a mocked report. Still local DB/filesystem-level, so Integration per level definitions; no external OS/network API dependency beyond local process I/O.
behaviors.test_description: Instantiate the real persistent queue implementation with capacity N using local test storage, enqueue N events successfully, then enqueue one more and assert a capacity rejection error/state without data corruption.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Rejected System: behavior can be reproduced with local in-process queue + local persistence; no real external network/subprocess boundary required.
